Echoing our offline discussion, the default SQLite implementation is run-sharded, with only the asset events getting mirrored into the index-connection (from the run-specific connections).  This means the step start / step failed events will never get returned by this subscription.

We are probably better off limiting to subscribing to asset-only events, and then using the asset planned event to subsequently subscribing to all the run-specific events.

I'm +1 on this PR, just left one small suggestion. I've also provided some more answers and information below.

<hr>

> I just thought that this could also be achieved by defining a new client fixture that just creates a client from the cluster fixture defined here. This would involve minimal changes to the existing code (only the client that the tests receive would be different, but the code would stay the same). Should I go this way?

If this is just for the sake of code organization but wouldn't have any functional or performance benefit, I'd rather not. Using fixtures of fixtures makes me (maybe irrationally) nervous. I'm ok with the changes you've made in this PR, passing in a cluster and then creating a client from it. I'm also ok with using the client context manager to handle teardowns instead of `Client.close()`.

<hr>

In the future, when you open a PR or issue that comes from some previous discussion, please link that discussion. That helps anyone who finds this PR from search to find all of the relevant context, and helps reviewers who might not have been part of that original conversation to learn what they need to give an effective review.

In this case, I'm linking https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/issues/3829#issuecomment-811115417.

<hr>

You don't have to close and re-open PRs on GitHub to re-trigger CI. In most projects, you can just push an empty commit. In LightGBM we have a slight preference for that because a new commit means new builds, which means it is possible to view the logs from the previous builds, which can be useful in debugging. Some CI services overwrite builds if you close and re-open. This is why, for example, only the most recent build of this PR is currently available on GitHub Actions, even though there have been two.

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/7608904/113497173-f2085580-94c6-11eb-9cf1-8ff5e7249a1a.png)

You can push an empty commit like this:

```shell
git commit --allow-empty -m "empty commit"
git push tests/cluster-fixture
```

This is looking great - thanks a ton for the many iterations! 

Regarding where to store these, after thinking about it some more it seems like the current options worth discussing are:
1. Add aggregate metrics to all workers.
2. Add aggregate metrics to 0th worker.
3. Change the entire pattern of passing reported metrics to use a more complex dictionary as opposed to the current list.

In the long run I do think we'll move towards (3), but it's out of scope for this PR. There are some additional considerations we'll need to think more about such as the general usability of Callbacks and integration with Tune (currently we only pass worker 0 results and don't support Train Callbacks, but we'll eventually need to support passing aggregate metrics to Tune). 

For (1) vs (2), the tradeoff is verbosity (e.g. if the user wants to log all results to a JSON file) vs. configurability (e.g. if the user wants to log only worker 2 results + aggregate results to TensorBoard). My initial hunch is to go with (2) and see if users request this type of customizability. @jwyyy @Yard1 thoughts?

Also cc @amogkam since this is worth considering for the API redesign.

This looks fine, just one thing to note though:

The ui urls and the service api urls are diverging a little bit, but they were already inconsistent. We don't have a formal interface around ui urls, which is something we may wish to address in future, so that people and systems can safely link to ui elements.

The queue.getArtifact URL path is:
`<ROOT_URL>/api/queue/v1/task/<TASK_ID>/runs/<RUN_ID>/artifacts/<ARTIFACT_PATH>`

The Task Inspector URL path for the live log artifact is now:
`<ROOT_URL>/tasks/<TASK_ID>/runs/<RUN_ID>/logs/live/<ARTIFACT_NAME>`

As you see, the path elements had already diverged (e.g. `task` -> `tasks`, `artifact` -> `logs/live`).

There is quite a lot going on here, not all of it is clear to me. I have looked over the main changes and most of it seems to be in order. In summary, the main changes introduced in this pull request are:
1. Added logic to simplify heap assembly when packets are expected to be received in order.
2. Streamlined the stream configuration option to two objects: a StreamConfig() and RingStreamConfig() object

I also spent some time looking at the examples and the documentation. I have some thoughts on what could be added there. They are not related to the changes made in this review, so consider them as suggestions.
1. When reading the documentation for the first time, it is difficult to figure out how one would start a SPEAD2 project. The documentation contains a detailed API, but it requires a bit of legwork to figure out how all the different bits fit together(eg: You know you have a memory pool and a thread pool, but then you need to go over the rest of the docs to figure out where they fit in). The examples in spead2/examples serve to show how all the parts interact, but I could not find any reference to these examples in the docs. If you install spead2 using "pip install", then it is quite likely that you wont even know that the examples exist. To make this more accessible to first time users, I would recommend explicitly mentioning the examples somewhere in the documentation.
2. There is a test_send_asyncio.py script in the examples. Is it worth having a test_receive_asyncio.py script? In recv.py in katfgpu there is a AsyncRingbuffer class. Was this specific to katfgpu or would this be something someone using standard spead2 would need to consider?

Thanks for your responses and the changes! I'm glad that we'll consider supporting this in a way that's more similar to `weight` and `init_score`.

I still think this PR requires some significant changes, however. I left some comments on specific files, but still have 2 high-level concerns that haven't been addressed:

1. I strongly believe passing positions via file should flow through a configuration value like `positions_file` or something, instead of assuming a specific naming convention and (in my opinion, unnecessarily) having the restriction that you can only use positions from a file if your training data is also in a file.

2. I do believe that the interface should be that users provide integers, not strings of the form `pos_{i}`. To be honest @metpavel , I did not understand some of the things you said in https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/pull/5929#issuecomment-1633729902.

For example, how is it that asking someone to provide `pos_1, pos_2, ...` is somehow avoiding the need to "perform the mapping to integers beforehand"?

And how does that string contract make it "easier for a user to specify a position of an item in complex layouts (let's say 2D)"?

Even beyond these things... integers will be easier for users to produce (no need to append this special `"pos_"` prefix and cast to string), and will result in smaller file sizes (which might be significant for large datasets).

When I saw https://github.com/learningequality/kolibri/issues/10930, I was wondering if there's really a need to be changing the behavior, because I'd actually expect being taken to an error page after clicking something in the disconnected state/overlay and generally speaking, I consider letting people reload the page themselves a good practice when compared to doing it automatically (to prevent losing work in progress or just because it's quite intrusive and unexpected UX). In this particular case, because there is the check ensuring it happens only during and shortly after the loading phase, it seems to be safe to do, and when testing, I had an impression it improves the experience (definitely better then being stuck in the empty loading page).

I agree with @nucleogenesis that this won't address local loading states, but I think it's questionable if that's actually needed and what's expected UX.

Overall, I think it'd be best to clarify our expectations towards global vs loading (including errors) UX in general as lately there were more discussions about that.

Noted some items needed for getting this ready for making this repository its home 😃 . Most of it just adding missing files (see the [template project](https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python/tree/main/sdk/template/azure-template) for the general idea):

1. Add a `dev_requirements.txt` file which is used for ci/testing. I think the following contents are probably fine for now.
    ```
    -e ../../../tools/azure-sdk-tools
    -e ../../../tools/azure-devtools
    ```
    along with the rest of your test requirements.

2. Add a `CHANGELOG.md` file. Formatting guidance can be found [here](https://azure.github.io/azure-sdk/policies_releases.html#changelog-guidance), but pretty much formatted like:
    ```
    # Release History

    ## 1.0.0b12 (Unreleased)

    ### Features Added

    ### Breaking Changes

    ### Bugs Fixed

    ### Other Changes
    ```

3. Add an `sdk_packaging.toml` file. Can just contain:
    ```
    [packaging]
    auto_update = false
    ```

4. Add a `pyproject.toml` file with a section like:
    ```
    [tool.azure-sdk-build]
    type_check_samples = false
    verifytypes = false
    pyright = false
    ```
   Can also do `pylint = false` if you want to disable that too, but better to see if you can get it to pass first. Similarly with `mypy = false`.

5. Add an empty `py.typed` file to the project (i.e., `./azure/monitor/opentelemetry/py.typed`)

5. Add a `MANIFEST.in` file. I think contents would be:
    ```
    recursive-include tests *.py
    recursive-include samples *.py
    include *.md
    include LICENSE
    include azure/__init__.py
    include azure/monitor/__init__.py
    include azure/monitor/opentelemetry/py.typed
    ```

6. Can remove `setup.cfg` as it's not needed.

7. Add the project to the Artifacts list in [ci.yml](https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python/blob/main/sdk/monitor/ci.yml#L32)

6. Update `https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python/blob/main/shared_requirements.txt` with package names that are included in your setup.py dependencies but not listed there.

Unfortunately I think the change will not be that easy.

* First, we are now changing the behavior for all endpoint strategies. The `domain` strategy *relies* on host matching. So removing host matching completely will break that, since now a request to 
`http://domain1.es.localhost.localstack.cloud/_cluster/health` is equivalent to `http://domain2.es.localhost.localstack.cloud/_cluster/health` and `http://localhost/_cluster/health`, etc.
So the thing that you're doing (using the url's path as base_url, which I think is very clever!) should only be done for the `path` endpoint strategy. Doing that check based on global configuration right in `EdgeProxiedOpensearchCluster` is obviously not great. So need a solution for that.

On typing:
- We don't need checks inside the `schedule_execution_time_iterator` and `cron_string_iterator`, because they're not public-facing. If they had them in the past, it's because they were written before we started mypy-typing our codebase.

- We typically prefer `Sequence` to `List`, because it indicates that the function won't mutate the value, and it's [covariant](https://blog.magrathealabs.com/pythons-covariance-and-contravariance-b422c63f57ac).
